messages.content:
### Summary: *An Essay Concerning Human Understanding* (Vol. I)

John Locke’s *An Essay Concerning Human Understanding* is a foundational text of empiricism. In this volume, Locke seeks to understand the limits and origins of human knowledge, ultimately challenging the existence of "innate" principles.

#### **Key Points**

*   **The Rejection of Innate Ideas:** Locke argues against the theory that humans are born with "innate" speculative or practical principles pre-imprinted on the mind. He posits that the mind is a *tabula rasa* (blank slate) and that all knowledge is derived from experience ([S.1]).
*   **The Origins of Knowledge:** 
    *   Knowledge originates from two primary sources: **sensation** (external objects affecting our senses) and **reflection** (the mind observing its own operations) ([S.1]).
    *   Ideas are classified as either "simple" (the basic building blocks) or "complex" (the mind’s combinations of simple ideas) ([S.1], [S.2]).
*   **The Purpose of the Essay:**
    *   Locke describes his role as an "under-labourer" clearing away "rubbish"—specifically the use of unintelligible, affected, and technical language that obscures true understanding ([S.5]).
    *   He intends for his writing to be clear and accessible, hoping readers will think for themselves rather than accepting ideas "on trust" from others ([S.3], [S.4]).
*   **Revision and Clarification:** 
    *   Locke displays a commitment to intellectual honesty, admitting that he is willing to revise his own conclusions if evidence suggests he was mistaken, such as his adjustments regarding the nature of the "Will" and "Liberty" ([S.5]).
    *   He clarifies his stance on "virtue and vice," noting that he describes them not as arbitrary, but as subject to the rules of reputation and the Law of Nature, which determine how societies denominate actions ([S.5], [S.6]).

#### **Core Philosophy**
The central project of the text is to shift philosophy away from abstract, speculative doctrine and toward a scientific, observational study of how the human understanding interacts with the world. By removing "false foundations" like innate ideas, Locke believes we can better advance human knowledge ([S.5]).

Lesson 1 — Essay Concerning Human Understanding, Vol. 1 - Presentation10 slides
Locke's Essay: Challenging Innate Ideas
  • Critique of innate speculative and practical principles.
  • Asserting that knowledge originates solely from experience.

This document contains the introductory sections of John Locke’s *An Essay Concerning Humane Understanding*. The work outlines Locke's project to examine the origins, certainty, and extent of human knowledge. The key points are summarized below:

**The Motivation for the Essay**
Locke explains that the work originated from a casual discussion among friends who found themselves unable to resolve certain philosophical difficulties. Locke concluded that they were attempting to answer complex questions without first examining the capabilities of the human mind. Consequently, he set out to determine what objects the understanding is—and is not—fitted to handle [S.3].

**Core Philosophical Objectives**
Locke seeks to move away from "innate principles" (arguing that knowledge is not innate, but derived) and focus instead on the origin of ideas. He provides a systematic study of how ideas (simple and complex) are formed through sensation and reflection. He also emphasizes the importance of clear language, noting that much of what passes for "deep learning" is often obscured by the abuse of words and unintelligible terminology [S.1, S.2, S.5].

**Locke’s Role and Methodology**
Locke modestly describes himself as an "under-labourer" who aims to clear the ground of "rubbish" that hinders the advancement of true knowledge. He acknowledges that his writing style may include repetitions, which he attributes to the intermittent, long-term nature of the writing process and his desire to make complex ideas accessible to the average reader. He explicitly warns that the book is intended for those interested in self-examination rather than for those who already consider themselves masters of the subject [S.3, S.4, S.5].

**Dedication and Purpose**
The essay is dedicated to the Earl of Pembroke, whom Locke respects for his commitment to truth and his own intellectual pursuits. Locke concludes that the ultimate goal of the work is utility; he hopes to strip away the vanity and ignorance inherent in academic jargon to foster a clearer, more practical understanding of the world [S.2, S.3, S.5].

This section explores the origin of ideas and the nature of the human soul, specifically focusing on the theory that the soul is not in a state of constant, conscious thought.

Key points from the provided text:

The Origin of Ideas
All human ideas originate from two sources: Sensation (external objects affecting our senses) and Reflection (the mind’s internal observation of its own operations). The understanding possesses no ideas that do not derive from these two paths (p. 34). Children begin life with few ideas and gradually accumulate them through sensory experience; reflection on internal processes typically occurs later, as it requires conscious attention (p. 34).

The Necessity of Consciousness
The author rejects the popular philosophical view that the soul is always thinking. He argues that having an idea and being conscious of it are the same thing (p. 35). If the soul were to think without the individual being conscious of it, that thinking would be practically useless and would lead to the absurd conclusion that the sleeping man and the waking man are two distinct persons or identities (p. 35, 36).

The Flaw in Constant Thinking
The author argues that there is no proof for the assertion that the soul thinks during dreamless sleep. Suggesting that the soul thinks but the memory fails to retain it is, according to the author, a groundless hypothesis (p. 36). Furthermore, if the soul thought independently of the body, these thoughts should theoretically be more rational and use innate ideas; however, dreams are typically incoherent, and there is no evidence of the soul recalling "native" or pre-existing ideas (p. 37).

Conclusion on Mental Activity
The author concludes that thinking is an operation of the soul, much like motion is an operation of the body, rather than its essence. Since thinking is not the soul’s constant state, it is unnecessary to believe the soul is always active, especially when such activity produces no useful memory, knowledge, or awareness for the person (p. 35, 36).

This excerpt explores the cognitive processes of the mind as defined by John Locke, detailing how we transition from simple sensations to complex knowledge.

### The Operations of the Mind
The mind functions through several key operations:
1. Composition: Combining simple ideas into complex ones (p. 56).
2. Enlarging: Combining ideas of the same kind to create larger concepts, such as a dozen or a furlong (p. 56).
3. Naming: Assigning verbal signs to ideas fixed in memory (p. 56).
4. Abstraction: Separating particular ideas from their circumstances (time, place, etc.) to create general, universal representatives (p. 56-57).

### Human vs. Animal Cognition
The author posits a sharp distinction between human beings and brutes (animals). While animals possess senses and can retain specific complex ideas (like recognizing a master), they lack the faculty of abstraction. Animals essentially act upon particular ideas; they cannot form general ideas or use language, which is the defining characteristic that separates humans from beasts (p. 56-57).

### Idiots vs. Madmen
The text differentiates between these states based on cognitive failures:
- Idiots: Suffer from a lack of quickness, activity, and the ability to reason or form propositions (p. 57).
- Madmen: Retain the faculty of reasoning but join ideas incorrectly, leading them to argue logically from false premises (p. 57).

### Complex Ideas
The mind eventually constructs complex ideas from simple ones, which can be categorized into three groups:
1. Modes: Dependent ideas that do not exist by themselves (e.g., triangle, gratitude). These are split into simple modes (combinations of one type) and mixed modes (combinations of several types like beauty or theft) (p. 58-59).
2. Substances: Ideas representing particular things that subsist by themselves, such as a man or lead (p. 59).
3. Relations: Ideas formed by comparing one thing to another (p. 59).

### The Source of Knowledge
The author maintains that all knowledge originates from two sources: sensation (external) and reflection (internal operations). The mind is likened to a "dark room" that only receives light through these windows. Even abstract concepts like space, time, and infinity are ultimately constructed from these foundational simple ideas via the mind’s own active processes (p. 57-59).

This text explores the philosophical origins of our perceptions of time and duration, arguing that these concepts are derived from the mental experience of succession.

Key points include:

The Origin of Duration
Human beings perceive duration through the continuous train of ideas that pass through the mind while awake. Duration is the conceptual distance between successive ideas. When the mind stops producing a train of ideas, such as during deep sleep without dreaming, our perception of duration ceases (p. 66).

Succession vs. Motion
While people often associate time with external motion, the author argues that motion is only perceived as such when it creates a sequence of new ideas in the mind. If there is no train of ideas, we cannot perceive motion either. Consequently, duration is fundamentally a product of reflection upon the mind's own internal activity (p. 67).

The Nature of the Mental Train
The succession of ideas in the mind appears to have inherent bounds of quickness and slowness. If changes occur more rapidly than our mental processing speed—such as a bullet passing through a room—we fail to perceive succession. This minimal duration of a single, unvarying idea is defined as an "instant." Conversely, the mind cannot fixate on a single, invariable idea for an extended time because our thoughts naturally shift and vary (pp. 67-68).

The Concept of Time
Time is defined as duration marked and measured by specific periods or epochs. Because duration is fleeting and cannot be stored like physical dimensions (such as length or width), humans must rely on recurring, apparently equal periods to "measure" it. While astronomical cycles like the sun and moon are historically the standard, any regular, perceivable periodic event—such as seasonal changes or biological rhythms—could theoretically serve as a valid measure of time (pp. 68-69).

This text explores the formation of complex ideas through sensation and reflection, specifically focusing on "modes." The main points are as follows:

**Development of Ideas and Names**
The mind forms complex ideas by combining simple ones. While some simple ideas (like colors) often lack distinct names due to their subtle differences or lack of general utility, others (like motion or thinking) are modified into named, complex concepts. Names are often created to facilitate communication, particularly regarding professional operations or human interactions, which explains why specialized terminology exists in specific trades (p. 84).

**Modes of Thinking**
Thinking is identified as an action rather than the essence of the soul, evidenced by the mind’s ability to fluctuate in its intensity of attention—ranging from deep study to "reverie" or sleep. The text categorizes various modes of thought, such as:
* Sensation: The initial entry of an idea through the senses (p. 84).
* Remembrance and Recollection: The return of previously held ideas (p. 85).
* Contemplation and Attention: The sustained focus on ideas.
* Dreaming: Thinking that occurs when external senses are inactive (p. 85).

**Modes of Pleasure and Pain**
Pleasure and pain are fundamental simple ideas derived from sensation or reflection, functioning as the basis for defining "good" and "evil." Good is defined as that which increases pleasure or diminishes pain, while evil is that which produces pain or diminishes pleasure. These experiences serve as the hinges for human passions (p. 86):
* Love: The delight produced by a thing.
* Hatred: The pain produced by a thing.
* Desire: The uneasiness felt due to the absence of a desired good.
* Joy and Sorrow: Delights related to possession or pain related to loss.
* Hope and Fear: Expectations concerning future pleasure or pain.
* Anger and Envy: Specific modes involving judgment of injury or social comparison (p. 87).

Ultimately, the author posits that passions are not inherent, but are instead mental reactions to the presence or absence of pleasure and pain, which drive human effort and industry through the underlying necessity of relieving "uneasiness" (p. 87).

This chapter explores the origins of our ideas of "power," the nature of the will, and the meaning of liberty.

**The Idea of Power**
Locke defines "power" as the mind’s observation that things can change or be changed. We gain this idea by noticing alterations in objects (simple ideas changing) and reflecting on our own internal capacity to initiate action (pp. 88). Power is categorized as either active (the ability to make a change) or passive (the ability to receive a change) (pp. 88). While we observe passive power in all sensible things, Locke argues that our clearest understanding of active power comes from reflecting on our own minds—specifically our ability to initiate thought and motion (pp. 89).

**The Will and Volition**
The mind possesses two primary powers: the understanding (perception) and the will (the power to prefer or choose to begin, continue, or stop an action) (pp. 89). Locke clarifies that the "will" and "understanding" are not real, separate beings or agents within the soul, but are merely faculties or powers of the individual (pp. 89).

**Liberty and Necessity**
Liberty is defined as the power to act or forbear acting according to the mind's preference (pp. 89). A person is free if their actions (or omissions) result from their own volition; conversely, where such power is absent, the agent is under necessity (pp. 89-90). Locke emphasizes that liberty is not a property of the "will" itself, but of the person (the agent) (pp. 90-91).

**Critique of the Question: "Is the Will Free?"**
Locke famously dismisses the common debate over whether the human will is free as logically incoherent (pp. 90). He argues that:
1. The will is a power to prefer; liberty is the power to act according to that preference (pp. 91).
2. To ask if the will is free is to ask if a power has another power, which is grammatically and logically absurd (pp. 91).
3. Powers belong to agents (substances), not to other powers (pp. 91).

Ultimately, "will" signifies only the ability to choose, and "liberty" signifies the ability to execute that choice; therefore, liberty belongs to the agent rather than the faculty of the will (pp. 91).

This text, drawn from John Locke's philosophical inquiry, distinguishes between "mixed modes" and "substances," explaining how the human mind constructs complex ideas.

Key Concepts Summary:

The Nature of Mixed Modes
Mixed modes are complex ideas that do not necessarily represent things existing together in nature. Instead, they are created by the mind through an active process of combining simple ideas. These concepts, such as "hypocrisy," "murder," or "parricide," are primarily intellectual notions. Their unity is derived not from an external reality, but from the human act of grouping them and assigning a single name to the combination (p. 107).

Language and Social Construction
The primary reason for creating these combinations is the efficiency of communication. Because words are meant to facilitate social interaction, people develop names for specific clusters of ideas that are useful in their culture and daily lives. Consequently, languages differ because the customs and societal needs of different nations dictate which combinations of ideas are assigned names. This explains why some languages contain terms that have no direct equivalent in others, and why languages evolve over time as societal structures change (p. 107, 108).

Methods of Acquiring Mixed Modes
Individuals acquire these complex ideas in three primary ways: through direct observation of actions, through self-directed invention (creating a concept), or most commonly, through the explanation of names by others. All such complex ideas are ultimately reducible to the simple ideas that serve as their foundational materials (p. 108).

The Concept of Substance
In contrast to mixed modes, the idea of "substance" arises when our senses perceive a cluster of simple ideas that appear to coexist constantly. Because we cannot conceive how these qualities (like weight, color, or solidity) could exist on their own, our minds invent a "support" or "substratum" to hold them together. Locke argues that our actual knowledge of any substance is limited to this collection of observed qualities; the underlying "substance" itself remains an unknown "something" that we merely assume exists (p. 109, 110).

Summary of Intellectual Limitation
Locke concludes that whether we discuss actions or objects, we are ultimately working with simple ideas received from sensation or reflection. When we speak of a substance, we are simply referring to an unknown support for qualities we can perceive. We remain in the dark regarding the true essence of things, as our intellectual tools are restricted to naming and combining the simple, observable components of our experience (p. 108, 110).

This text explores the philosophical foundations of identity, distinguishing between substance, living organisms, and personal identity.

Key Concepts of Identity

1. Principium Individuationis (The Principle of Individuation): Identity is based on existence at a specific time and place. No two things of the same kind can exist in the same place at the same time [S.124].

2. Identity of Substances: The author categorizes three types of substances: God (eternal/unalterable), Finite Spirits (defined by their beginning in time/place), and Bodies (defined by being a particle of matter that does not change via addition or subtraction) [S.124].

3. Identity of Vegetables and Animals: Living organisms, such as plants and animals, maintain identity not through the permanence of their physical particles, but through the continuity of their life and organization. As particles are shed and replaced, the "one common life" sustains the entity's identity [S.125].

4. The Identity of Man: A human being is defined as an animal with a specific, organized physical form. This identity is maintained by the continuous participation in the same life, regardless of the constant flux of the body's material particles [S.125-126].

5. Personal Identity: This is distinct from substance or biological identity. A person is a "thinking intelligent being" capable of reason and reflection. Personal identity consists solely in consciousness—the ability for a self to perceive its own past thoughts and actions. This consciousness links past and present selves into one person, even if the underlying substance changes over time [S.126].

6. Substance vs. Person: The author argues that personal identity is not dependent on the stability of a single substance. Just as an animal remains the same despite changing material particles, a person remains the same so long as the same consciousness is present, even if the immaterial or material substance supporting that consciousness were theoretically changed or transferred [S.127].

This section of the text explores the nature of relations and moral concepts, emphasizing how human understanding is built upon "simple ideas" derived from sensation and reflection. The author categorizes these relations and the rules governing human morality into several key areas:

**The Nature of Relations**
The author identifies three primary types of relations:
1. Proportional Relations: These involve comparing specific qualities (such as being whiter or sweeter) between subjects.
2. Natural Relations: These arise from the origin or circumstances of subjects, such as biological kinship (father, son). These terms often reflect societal convenience rather than the objective reality of the subjects themselves.
3. Instituted or Voluntary Relations: These are grounded in human agreements, laws, or social structures (e.g., general, citizen). These are alterable and depend entirely on the will of individuals within a society.

**The Four Types of Moral Laws**
The text defines "moral good and evil" as the conformity or disagreement of voluntary actions with an established law. There are three primary rules, or "laws," to which individuals refer to judge the morality of an action:

1. Divine Law: Established by God, this determines whether an action is a sin or a duty. It serves as the ultimate "touchstone" of moral rectitude, enforced by the prospect of eternal rewards or punishments (pp. 133-134).
2. Civil Law: The rule set by the commonwealth to protect the society. It dictates whether an action is criminal or innocent, with enforcement carried out by the state via rewards or legal penalties (p. 133).
3. The Law of Opinion or Reputation: Also described as the "law of fashion," this is the tacit consent of society to praise certain actions as virtues and condemn others as vices. While this fluctuates by culture, it often echoes the divine law because societies naturally encourage actions that are socially beneficial (pp. 133-134).

**Conclusion on Moral Actions**
Ultimately, moral concepts are "mixed modes"—complex ideas comprised of simpler collections of ideas derived from experience. When we evaluate an action as "good" or "bad," we are not merely describing the action itself (an absolute, positive mode); we are evaluating the action in relation to one of the three aforementioned rules. Consequently, morality is fundamentally the study of how human actions align with or deviate from the standards set by these societal, civil, or divine authorities (pp. 134-135).

This section explores the nature of truth and falsehood in relation to ideas. The key points are summarized as follows:

The Core Nature of Truth
In strict philosophical terms, truth and falsehood belong only to propositions, not to ideas themselves. Because ideas are merely perceptions or "appearances" in the mind, they cannot be inherently true or false any more than a single word can be. When we label an idea as "true" or "false," we are implicitly making a judgment or a tacit proposition about that idea (p. 146).

Instances Where Ideas Are Labeled True or False
Ideas are only evaluated for truth or falsehood when the mind refers them to something external, such as:
1. Other men's ideas: When a person assumes their abstract idea (e.g., justice) aligns with the common usage or the idea held by others (p. 146).
2. Real existence: When a person assumes their idea corresponds to a real substance or entity in reality (p. 146).
3. Real essences: When a person incorrectly assumes their abstract idea captures the underlying nature or "essence" of a thing (p. 146).

Classification of Ideas and Falsehood
Simple Ideas: These are perceptions produced by external objects according to natural laws. Because they are the basic building blocks of distinction provided by nature, they cannot be false. Even if different people perceive the same object differently, the utility of the idea for distinguishing objects remains intact (p. 148).

Complex Ideas of Modes: These ideas, such as justice or frugality, are voluntary combinations of simple ideas. They are often called "false" when they fail to align with the customary definitions or the ideas held by other people using the same names (p. 147).

Complex Ideas of Substances: These are the only ideas that can be truly "false" regarding real existence. They are false when they group together simple ideas that do not coexist in nature (e.g., attributing the power of barking to the form of a horse) or when a defining characteristic is excluded by negation (p. 149).

Conclusion
Ultimately, all labels of truth or falsehood regarding ideas originate from the mind's judgment. Ideas viewed merely as internal representations cannot be false; they only become so when the mind makes an incorrect affirmation or negation while referring that idea to an external standard or to the usage of others (p. 149).
Here is a quiz for this lesson.

questions.question: According to the provided text, what motivated Locke to undertake the examination of the human understanding?
options: He wanted to challenge the scientific theories of Mr. Newton and Huygenius.
His friends struggled to resolve certain difficulties and realized they needed to understand the scope of their own faculties first.
He was commanded by the Earl of Pembroke to refute the existence of God.
He believed that innate ideas were the only reliable source of philosophical truth.
questions.explanation: Locke explains in the Epistle to the Reader that the project originated from discussions with friends who became stuck on a problem and realized they needed to examine what the understanding is fitted to deal with before continuing further inquiries.

questions.question: What is Locke's primary role as a philosopher in this essay, as he describes in the Epistle to the Reader?
options: To establish a new, complex system of metaphysical science.
To act as a master-builder of knowledge alongside Newton and Boyle.
To act as an 'under-labourer' in clearing the ground and removing 'rubbish' from the path to knowledge.
To create an exhaustive list of innate ideas that every human possesses from birth.
questions.explanation: Locke explicitly states that compared to master-builders like Newton and Boyle, his ambition is to be an 'under-labourer' who clears the ground and removes the 'rubbish' of vague, unintelligible terms that hinder knowledge.

questions.question: How does Locke view the use of 'uncouth, affected, or unintelligible terms' in philosophy?
options: As necessary tools for describing complex intellectual concepts.
As essential 'mysteries of science' that define true learning.
As covers for ignorance that hinder the progress of true knowledge.
As the only way to make philosophy acceptable for 'well-bred company'.
questions.explanation: Locke argues that overly complex or vague language is often used to mask a lack of clear thought, functioning as 'covers of ignorance' that prevent true understanding.

questions.question: According to the text, why does Locke choose to present his thoughts in a repetitive or long-winded manner?
options: He believes repetitive language is the most effective way to communicate complex ideas to different types of minds.
He is being paid by the publisher to increase the bulk of the book.
He believes that the more often a statement is repeated, the more likely it is to be factually true.
He wants the book to be longer than the works produced by his peers.
questions.explanation: Locke explains that because people learn and grasp ideas differently ('understandings... no less different than our palates'), he explains things in different ways to ensure that truth is accessible to everyone.

questions.question: What is the author's stance on publishing his work, according to the Epistle to the Reader?
options: He is highly eager to see his name in print to boost his reputation.
He would have been content leaving the work to his friends if he did not think it could be useful to others.
He considers publishing to be the ultimate goal of every intellectual pursuit.
He feels that his work is only fit for the most advanced scholars and ignores the general reader.
questions.explanation: Locke states, 'I have so little affection to be in print, that if I were not flattered this Essay might be of some use to others... I should have confined it to the view of some friends.'

cards.front: According to Book I of Locke's Essay, are there any innate speculative or practical principles?
cards.back: No. Locke argues against the doctrine of innate principles and ideas.

cards.front: What did Locke believe was the necessary first step before pursuing inquiries into abstract knowledge?
cards.back: To examine our own abilities and determine what objects our understandings are, or are not, fitted to deal with.

cards.front: How does Locke describe his own role in the advancement of knowledge?
cards.back: He describes himself as an 'under-labourer' whose task is to clear the ground and remove the 'rubbish' that hinders the advancement of knowledge.

cards.front: What does Locke identify as a major hindrance to true philosophy and knowledge?
cards.back: The use of uncouth, affected, or unintelligible terms, and the abuse of language which masks ignorance.

cards.front: What led Locke to write this Essay?
cards.back: A discussion with five or six friends that reached a standstill due to complex difficulties, leading them to agree to first examine the limits of the human understanding.

cards.front: How does Locke justify the presence of repetitions in his work?
cards.back: He explains that different readers have different understandings, and some objects need to be presented from different perspectives to be fully comprehended.

cards.front: What is the subject of Book II of Locke's Essay?
cards.back: Of Ideas, including their origin, simple ideas, complex ideas, and various operations of the mind.

cards.front: What is Locke's attitude toward 'new' opinions or truths?
cards.back: He argues that new truths should not be rejected simply because they are not common; like gold, their value is determined by trial and examination, not by fashion.

cards.front: According to the 'Epistle to the Reader', what source should a reader rely on for knowledge?
cards.back: They should rely on their own thoughts and independent judgment rather than taking opinions 'on trust' from others.

cards.front: How does Locke compare the act of searching for truth?
cards.back: He compares it to 'hawking and hunting', where the very pursuit itself provides constant delight and rewards the mind with discovery.

slides.title: Locke's Essay: Challenging Innate Ideas
bullets: Critique of innate speculative and practical principles.
Asserting that knowledge originates solely from experience.
slides.notes: John Locke challenges traditional views by arguing that the mind begins as a blank slate, devoid of innate concepts.

slides.title: The Origin of All Our Ideas
bullets: Ideas arrive via sensation or reflection (p. 34).
Sensation provides external sensible qualities.
Reflection reveals the mind’s internal operations.
Children gain knowledge gradually through experience (p. 34).
slides.notes: Everything in our understanding stems from either external sense perception or internal mental reflection.

slides.title: Composition and Abstraction
bullets: The mind uses composition to build complex ideas (p. 56).
Abstraction creates general representatives from particulars (p. 56).
Language uses general signs for these abstract ideas.
Humans uniquely possess the faculty of abstraction.
slides.notes: Locke explains how the mind transforms simple sensory data into complex, abstract, and general concepts.

slides.title: Duration, Number, and Infinity
bullets: Duration is derived from reflecting on successions of ideas.
Perception of time ceases when thoughts stop (p. 66).
Temporal awareness requires an active, waking mind.
Motion is perceived only through successive ideas (p. 66).
slides.notes: Our sense of time and eternity arises not from external motion, but from the internal train of our thoughts.

slides.title: Mixed Modes and Thinking
bullets: Mixed modes combine diverse simple ideas into complex entities.
Names for modes are often driven by social utility.
Thinking includes various modes like memory and attention (p. 84).
Reflection on these modes reveals the mind's operations.
slides.notes: Many of our conceptual categories are 'mixed modes' constructed by the mind to facilitate communication.

slides.title: Power and the Will
bullets: Power is the ability to make or receive change.
Active power is best understood through mental reflection.
Bodies only demonstrate passive power via motion.
The mind initiates motion through volition.
slides.notes: Locke distinguishes between passive reception of change and the active power of the mind and spirit.

slides.title: Complex Ideas and Substances
bullets: The mind actively constructs notions or 'mixed modes'.
Unity in complex ideas is provided by a name.
Language mirrors cultural customs and specific needs.
Definitions often explain combinations of simple ideas.
slides.notes: The mind gathers independent ideas into unified complexes, using names as tools to organize these groups.

slides.title: Identity and Diversity
bullets: Identity is determined by existence at a specific time.
Vegetable life depends on continued organization (p. 125).
Personal identity differs from mass of matter.
Principles of individuation are grounded in existence.
slides.notes: Identity is not merely about matter, but about organized life and the history of existence.

slides.title: Relations and Moral Truths
bullets: Relations compare ideas of origin or proportion.
Moral relations judge actions against a specific rule.
Instituted relations depend on human social agreement.
Language tracks social needs, not the nature of things.
slides.notes: Relations are intellectual connections made by the mind, especially important in moral and social contexts.

slides.title: Truth, Falsehood, and Association
bullets: Truth and falsehood properly belong only to propositions.
Ideas are true if they conform to reality.
The mind classifies ideas to expedite knowledge.
We often mistakenly rely on names for essences.
slides.notes: While ideas themselves are just appearances, they become 'true' or 'false' when the mind makes judgments about them.
